episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [The Marginalian] Legendary Cellist Pablo Casals, at Age 93, on Creative Vitality and How Working with Love Prolongs Your Life and [New Atlas] Daddy longlegs seen catching and eating frogs in astonishing first, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can small rural arts organizations sustainably preserve twenty years of cultural history without institutional resources or ongoing funding? Topics include: Digital archival tools and their declining costs, Sovereignty and community control of cultural records (Mukurtu example), Organizational coordination failures in nonprofit sectors, Peer-to-peer knowledge transfer models (Christmas bird count analogy), Timing archival efforts to natural milestones (anniversary events).

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Central Cariboo Arts & Culture Society

articles.title: Tod Maffin - Official Web Site
articles.summary: Canadian culture commentator, agency owner, business journalist.

discussion.central_question: How can small rural arts organizations sustainably preserve twenty years of cultural history without institutional resources or ongoing funding?
discussion.riley_position: Digital tools have made archiving affordable enough for volunteer organizations to implement basic systems immediately; a single coordinated effort tied to a milestone event could establish practices that spread peer-to-peer across the region without new funding.
riley_key_evidence: Cloud storage, AI-assisted transcription, and automated photo tagging have reduced archival costs from requiring a full-time archivist to a few volunteer hours monthly
Internet Archive provides free hosting for community media with no ongoing cost to organizations
Central Cariboo Arts & Culture Society could create a standardized template once and share it across the region's arts organizations
discussion.casey_position: Technical solutions don't address the real problems: governance decisions, continuity risks, organizational bandwidth limitations, and the coordination failures that plague small nonprofit partnerships.
casey_key_evidence: Mukurtu's success required purpose-built design with sovereignty as a core requirement from inception, not bolted-on tools
Twenty years of volunteer festival records exist in fragmented formats across different people; completeness cannot be assumed
Coordination between separate volunteer boards with competing priorities consistently fails at the second meeting when unpaid work is revealed
discussion.resolution: Casey conceded that a backup with known risks beats no backup, and agreed that a shared regional template approach differs meaningfully from traditional grant-based models. They agreed the real question is whether local organizations actually have bandwidth to implement even a simple solution, and suggested posing this directly to the community.
topics_covered: Digital archival tools and their declining costs
Sovereignty and community control of cultural records (Mukurtu example)
Organizational coordination failures in nonprofit sectors
Peer-to-peer knowledge transfer models (Christmas bird count analogy)
Timing archival efforts to natural milestones (anniversary events)
